---
title: "Course Materials And Git Workflow"
subtitle: "How to update without losing your work"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

# Git

The main source of course materials is the GitHub repository: [https://github.com/erikwestlund/ai-tools-2026]

At the start of each class, we will together open a terminal in the course folder and run:


```bash
git pull
```

To get updated materials.

# Saving Work

Save your own files only in:

```text
practice/work/
```

This folder is ignored by Git. 

Instructor-provided practice tasks live in `practice/tasks/`. That folder updates with `git pull`.

Do not edit course-owned files in `practice/tasks/`, `modules/`, `assignments/`, or `data/`. This will break the ability to update with `git pull`.

# Why We Use Git

Git is the industry standard software for version control. You should probably be using it for your analysis work. 

The course is short and live. I may update examples, add clarifications, fix links, or release the next day's materials after class. Git lets you receive those updates with one command while keeping your own work separate.

For these reasons, please to try to use Git in this course.

# First Setup

Clone the student-facing repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/erikwestlund/ai-tools-2026.git
cd ai-tools-2026
```

Then open the folder in RStudio, Positron, VS Code, or your preferred environment.

# Where To Work

Use `practice/work/` for your notebooks, scripts, rendered HTML files, notes, and assignment drafts.

Files in `practice/work/` are ignored by Git. That means `git pull` should not overwrite them. If you want to edit a task from `practice/tasks/`, copy it into `practice/work/` first.

Course-owned folders may change during updates:

- `modules/`
- `assignments/`
- `data/`
- `practice/tasks/`

You can read files in those folders, but do not save your own work there.

# If `git pull` Complains

If Git says your local changes would be overwritten, you probably edited a course-owned file by accident.

First, see what changed:

```bash
git status
```

Then save your local changes temporarily:

```bash
git stash
```

Update the course materials:

```bash
git pull
```

If you need the changes you stashed, try to restore them:

```bash
git stash pop
```

If `git stash pop` creates conflicts, stop and ask for help. Do not keep running random Git commands.

**Tip:** AI agents are very good at debugging git safely.

# ZIP Download Fallback

If you cannot use Git, you can download a fresh ZIP copy from GitHub:

1. Go to <https://github.com/erikwestlund/ai-tools-2026>.
2. Click the green **Code** button.
3. Click **Download ZIP**.
4. Unzip the new copy.
5. Copy your old `practice/` folder into the new copy.

Do not overwrite your old folder until you have copied out your `practice/` files.

# Emergency Reset

If your course folder becomes badly tangled:

1. Copy `practice/` somewhere safe, such as your Desktop.
2. Delete or rename the broken course folder.
3. Clone the repository again.
4. Copy your saved `practice/` files back into the new course folder.
